title: Experts react: What a President Abelardo de la Espriella means for Colombia and beyond
gist: Atlantic Council experts read right-wing outsider Abelardo de la Espriella's razor-thin (0.94 percent) Colombian presidential win as a pivot back toward the US and away from China, a likely catalyst for returning foreign investment after FDI fell 33 percent under Petro, but constrained by a contested result, no legislative majority, and a radical 40-percent state-cut, fracking-resumption agenda.

insights.claim: De la Espriella's win signals a return to close US-Colombia ties, prioritizing security and intelligence cooperation to dismantle armed groups like the ELN, FARC dissidents, and Tren de Aragua crossing the Colombia-Venezuela border.
insights.speaker: Jason Marczak
insights.so_what: A pro-US security pivot reshapes regional counter-narcotics and Venezuela-border risk; watch cabinet picks for confirmation.
insights.quote: a welcome return to the historically close relationship with the United States
insights.locator: Expect closer alignment with the US

insights.claim: De la Espriella won by less than one percentage point, the closest presidential election in Colombia's history, after polls showed him surging late.
insights.speaker: Jason Marczak
insights.so_what: A wafer-thin mandate caps how aggressively the agenda can be pushed and raises contestation risk.
insights.quote: he won by less than one percentage point—the closest presidential election in Colombia's history
insights.locator: Despite polls showing de la Espriella surging late

insights.claim: The incoming government will take a more skeptical approach to Chinese investment, especially in critical infrastructure, reversing Petro's embrace of China as a US alternative while staying pragmatic toward Beijing.
insights.speaker: Jason Marczak
insights.so_what: A China pullback could slow Chinese infrastructure deals in Colombia and redirect contracts toward US firms.
insights.quote: The incoming government will bring a more skeptical approach toward Beijing, especially when it comes to investment in critical infrastructure.
insights.locator: Outgoing President Gustavo Petro also actively sought

insights.claim: Distancing Colombia from China risks reducing Chinese infrastructure investment, and it is unclear whether the US will supplement financial support to offset the lost Chinese business.
insights.speaker: Sergio Guzman
insights.so_what: The realignment carries a financing gap; investors should watch whether US capital actually fills the China-sized hole.
insights.quote: It is also unclear if the United States will supplement its financial and economic support to Colombia
insights.locator: It is very likely that de la Espriella will be pressured

insights.claim: Colombian FDI fell consistently under Petro and by 2025 was down 33 percent from 2022, amid persistent deficits, a suspended fiscal rule, and skyrocketing coca production.
insights.speaker: Kristie Pellecchia Loiacono
insights.so_what: Quantifies the capital-flight baseline a pro-business government would need to reverse; sizes the recovery opportunity.
insights.quote: Colombian foreign direct investment dropped consistently under Petro, and by 2025 was down 33 percent from 2022
insights.locator: As a result, Colombian foreign direct investment dropped

insights.claim: In 2025 the IMF suspended Colombia's $9.8 billion credit line over fiscal concerns and two of three major agencies downgraded the country to high-yield, cutting off pockets of capital.
insights.speaker: Kristie Pellecchia Loiacono
insights.so_what: Loss of investment-grade access is the concrete fiscal damage the new government must repair to restore bond-market access.
insights.quote: the International Monetary Fund suspended Colombia's $9.8 billion line of credit over fiscal concerns

insights.claim: Technocrat VP-elect Jose Manuel Restrepo, trusted by international finance for his pragmatism, is the signal markets will watch for a return of capital alongside a coherent energy strategy.
insights.speaker: Kristie Pellecchia Loiacono
insights.so_what: Restrepo is the credibility anchor; his role and cabinet picks are the near-term catalyst for FDI return.
insights.quote: Restrepo is trusted by the international finance and business community, and he is known for his pragmatism.

insights.claim: The Colombian peso swung from an all-time low of 5,118 per US dollar to a 2026 high near 3,440 per dollar, adding challenges for investors.
insights.speaker: Kristie Pellecchia Loiacono
insights.so_what: Extreme FX volatility is a direct trading and hedging consideration for Colombia exposure.
insights.quote: hitting an all-time high of 5,118 per US dollar and then rallying in 2026 (hitting a low of 3,440 per US dollar)
insights.locator: The Colombian peso also had a wild ride

insights.claim: De la Espriella secured 12.9 million votes with a 0.94 percent margin (under 250,000 votes), and Cepeda is contesting the result, likely triggering a recount and a tense transition.
insights.speaker: Sergio Guzman
insights.so_what: A contested, narrow result creates near-term political and market uncertainty during the transition window.
insights.quote: his narrow 0.94 percent margin (under 250,000 votes) over Cepeda has triggered unprecedented institutional uncertainty
insights.locator: De la Espriella's narrow win means

insights.claim: Lacking a legislative majority, de la Espriella must build coalitions to advance a radical pro-business plan to cut the state apparatus by 40 percent, resume fracking, and eliminate some taxes.
insights.speaker: Sergio Guzman
insights.so_what: Fracking resumption and deep state cuts are concrete, sector-moving policies but execution depends on coalition-building.
insights.quote: a radical, pro-business economic shock plan that includes cutting the state apparatus by 40 percent, resuming fracking, and eliminating some taxes
insights.locator: Once in office, de la Espriella will face

insights.claim: Governing via unilateral executive decrees and states of exception risks legal battles with the Constitutional Court and State Council and sustained social mobilization from Petro's base.
insights.speaker: Sergio Guzman
insights.so_what: High-friction governance raises policy-reversal and unrest risk that could undercut the investment thesis.
insights.quote: setting up future legal battles with the Constitutional Court and the State Council
